text: Today I'll be presenting a robotic tap approach of a superior lumbar hernia repair. This is my first lumbar hernia repair. According to the literature, there's only within three hundred or so cases that have been reported since like the seventeen hundreds, which seems very low, especially with all the presentations that are placed onto IHC, but I was pretty excited to see this.

So this is a fifty-five-year-old male who presented to me with both an umbilical bulge and a left lateral wall bulge. He was more symptomatic from the umbilical bulge, but I knew that I didn't want to fix the umbilical hernia first before I tackled this repair, so I told him that we would fix the lumbar hernia first and then once he had healed up we can do the umbilical hernia.

The CT scan displays a superior lumbar hernia containing retroperitoneal fat, bordered by the twelfth rib, internal oblique, and quadratus lumborum, with the latissimus dorsi muscle forming the roof. A small fat-containing umbilical hernia is also visible.

The intraoperative image displays diaphragm fibers and bare muscle in the upper left corner, with transversalis fascia visible on the right. The small hernia defect is labeled, with underlying iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves. The twelfth subcostal nerve and lateral femoral cutaneous nerve may also be present. Retroperitoneal fat and kidney are medialized in the left portion, while the quadratus lumborum is visible in the lower right corner. The psoas muscle is more medial but not clearly shown.

The patient is positioned in a right lateral decubitus position for the procedure. The surgeon uses the OptiView technique to enter the abdomen, adjusting the patient to a full lateral position. The first port is placed a hand's breadth away from the subcostal margin, with two additional ports positioned approximately eight centimeters apart, forming a triangulation towards the left upper quadrant. The hernia site is visibly marked preoperatively.

The surgical area is exposed to allow for external pressure application if needed. The surgeon begins creating a flap in the region where the hernia is suspected, though it is obscured by the colon and retroperitoneal fat. The diaphragm muscle is visible, and the surgeon removes extraperitoneal fat to enhance visualization of the underlying muscle.

The surgeon begins flap dissection in the left lower quadrant, revealing the transversalis muscle with intact fascia. The diaphragm muscle fibers are visible, oriented differently and bare. The assistant applies external pressure on the abdomen to better visualize the hernia, which is positioned lower than expected.

The surgeon approaches the hernia site, revealing a significant amount of retroperitoneal fat and the quadratus lumborum muscle. The subcostal, ilioinguinal, and iliohypogastric nerves are noted to be in close proximity to the hernia, necessitating careful dissection to avoid nerve injury. The quadratus lumborum is clearly visible, and the surgeon aims to maintain proximity to this muscle while achieving the 'volcano sign' before proceeding with hernia reduction.

The surgeon applies downward traction on the hernia defect, which is smaller than anticipated, while using electrocautery to dissect around the area. A significant amount of retroperitoneal fat is visible within the defect, and the surgeon exercises caution due to potential nerve presence inferiorly.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope to improve visualization of the hernia, while the assistant applies external pressure to help reduce the retroperitoneal fat. The fat is gradually extracted from the hernia site, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon uses their left hand to hold back retroperitoneal fat while performing dissection with one hand. Two nerves, the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal, are visible. The hernia defect measures approximately one centimeter by two centimeters, with light yellow fat herniating into the defect. The surgeon transitions to a thirty-degree scope and uses a cigar for retraction, aiming to find the plane and cut Dorotis Fascia while medializing the kidney.

The surgeon and assistant identify the plane for dissection, marking the area to ensure proper positioning. The surgeon aims to achieve at least five centimeters of mesh overlap inferior to the hernia, with the mesh ideally covering the quadratus lumborum and extending onto the psoas muscle, which begins to come into view.

The surgeon measures the hernia, confirming its size as approximately one by two centimeters, and prepares a fifteen by fifteen centimeter heavyweight barred polypropylene mesh for closure. The inferior side is cleaned to ensure clear visualization of the fascia before closing the defect with a zero absorbable V-lock suture using a GS-II needle. The defect is closed smoothly and effectively.

The surgeon inserts a fifteen-centimeter heavyweight mesh through an eight-millimeter inferior port, encountering difficulty as it passes through the trocar, resulting in a small tear in the mesh. The mesh is positioned over the hernia defect, surrounded by retroperitoneal fat, ensuring it does not clamshell to prevent recurrence. A small defect in the mesh is visible.

The surgeon uses one hand to secure the heavyweight mesh superiorly to the transversalis fascia, while maneuvering the retroperitoneal fat and kidney to ensure proper placement. The mesh's opacity makes it challenging to visualize underlying structures, prompting the surgeon to repeatedly check the location of the hernia defect and nearby nerves before placing stay sutures.

The surgeon places several sutures superiorly and a figure-eight suture in a small rent in the heavyweight mesh. The mesh is maneuvered to sit well without securing it to the diaphragm to avoid bleeding from the exposed muscle. The quadratus lumborum muscle is visible, and the mesh is sutured to it.

The surgeon checks the position of nerves to avoid placing stay sutures on them, then tucks the corner of the mesh over the psoas muscle. The retroperitoneal fat is mostly removed, and the mesh is laid down. As the surgeon releases the retraction, the kidney and retroperitoneal fat fall back over the mesh, securing it in place. Two six-inch 3-0 V-lock sutures are used to close the peritoneal flap, starting caudally and superiorly, meeting in the middle. The peritoneum appears healthy, allowing for easy closure without holes.

But that completes the case. Thank you so much for watching. This was a fun case for me. I don't get to see this anatomy a lot. And early on in my career, so excited to get a lumbar hernia and hopefully I'll get some more and I'll be able to show my progress here. But I really want to thank Dr. David Laurier and Dr. Conrad, Dr. Conrad Ballester and Dr. Sabrina Drexel for their videos that they've posted before because they helped prepare me for this case and kind of gave me the confidence to do this case. So thanks everybody. I appreciate your critiques and comments.
